inline_comments.message: I think for classes (different than interfaces) is better to put private/protected/public.  Otherwise you get hybrid mix of default data access if you don't that is not specifically any of those, but may be that's what you're intending, not sure.
Yeah good point - I intend to work on this class this weekend. I think I want to move it to policy/models even though for the moment it is for Xacml PDP. Other teams could use the code for API calls.

Not sure this is applicable, since a test. But just in case, Jim had commented earlier that onar doesn't like it when public methods throw more than one exception type https://gerrit.onap.org/r/#/c/78789/2/controlloop/templates/template.demo.clc/src/test/java/org/onap/policy/template/demo/clc/ControlLoopCoordinationTest.java@98

Is this rule meant to be just an example, or also to modify the root policy's combining algorithm result?

For example, if a single other policy was added to the root set and that policy evaluated to NotApplicable, then the output of the root policy as currently written would be Permit as per the combining algorithm true table for deny-overrides at https://www.axiomatics.com/blog/understanding-xacml-combining-algorithms/. However, w/o this default Permit rule, the output would have been NotApplicable.

Same question for RootMonitoringPolicy.xml

I want to avoid Not Applicable in either of these policies. I think the root monitoring one is set for now. But the guard I only just started on. Again, we could skin this cat many ways. Let's just nail down the most likely one this week - probably what was done in Casablanca.
